summary: Jeff Atwood explores the Fermi paradox—the contradiction between the high probability of extraterrestrial life and our complete lack of evidence for it. He draws a parallel to the absence of time travelers as similar evidence against the possibility of time travel. After surveying the Great Filter theory's explanations for why civilizations might fail to reach interstellar capability, Atwood proposes his own theory: advanced life doesn't build bigger, it builds smaller. Just as computers evolved from room-sized machines to pocket devices, truly advanced civilizations may have miniaturized themselves into the spaces between atoms and moments, becoming invisible to our crude detection methods.
key_points: The Fermi paradox presents a compelling logical puzzle: given the universe's age and size, Earth should have been visited by now
The absence of time travelers from the future may similarly suggest time travel is impossible
The Great Filter theory proposes that some stage of evolution is nearly impossible to pass, explaining the silence
Advanced technology trends toward miniaturization, not expansion—smaller is faster, more efficient, and requires less energy
Truly advanced civilizations may have no need to rearrange stars when they can engineer at the nanoscale
money_quotes: With a 13 billion year head start on evolution, why haven't any other forms of life in the universe contacted us by now?
It seems, to me at least, clear evidence that time travel is not possible, given the enormous amount of time behind us.
Like computers themselves, things don't get larger. They get smaller. And faster. And so does intelligent life.
Why build planet-size anything when the real action is in the small things?
Large is inefficient and unnecessary. Look at the history of computers: from giant to tiny and tinier.
It could be that it's the engineering of the small, rather than the large, that is inevitable.
If we can barely see far in the universe as is, there's no way we could possibly see into the infinite space and time between.
key_insight: Advanced civilizations may become invisible not because they've died out, but because they've evolved beyond the large-scale engineering we're looking for—miniaturizing into the infinite spaces between atoms and moments where we cannot detect them.
